v.t: Flourishing of Ancient Greek olive oil industry and Mediterranean trade
v.s: Olive oil was the main cooking oil and lighting fuel in daily life of ancient Greeks, and was widely used in religious rituals, athletic competitions (anointing the body), and foreign trade. City-states like Athens accumulated great wealth through olive oil exports, promoting the development of trade networks in the Aegean and Mediterranean regions. Olive cultivation and pressing techniques became an important economic pillar of the Greek world.

v.t: Athenian democracy maturely operating after Cleisthenes' reforms
v.s: Cleisthenes' reforms in 508 BC established the foundation of Athenian democracy, and by 500 BC the system was operating stably. The Council of Five Hundred was selected by lot, the Ecclesia (citizens' assembly) held supreme power, and ostracism was used to prevent the return of tyranny. This system granted all citizens (except women and slaves) the right to participate in political decision-making, becoming the origin of Western democratic politics.

v.t: Popularity of the Chiton garment in ancient Greece
v.s: Ancient Greeks commonly wore a garment called 'Chiton', made from a rectangular piece of linen or wool fabric fastened at the shoulders with pins and belted at the waist. The Doric chiton was made of wool, simple and heavy, while the Ionic chiton used linen and was lighter and more flowing. This garment design reflected the ancient Greek aesthetic pursuit of naturalness, harmony and elegance, and profoundly influenced later Western clothing.

v.t: Persian Empire's Royal Road system and courier network perfected
v.s: Persian Emperor Darius I built the 'Royal Road', about 2,500 km long, from Susa to Sardis, with 111 stations equipped with messengers and fast horses. This royal road system allowed the imperial center to quickly transmit decrees and intelligence; messengers could cross the empire in seven days. The courier network also facilitated trade and military mobilization, and was one of the most advanced communication systems of the ancient world.

The Greek city-state of Miletus in Asia Minor, dissatisfied with the high-handed rule of the Persian Empire and its policy of supporting tyrants, launched an armed revolt under the leadership of Aristagoras, uniting the Ionian city-states. The rebels captured and burned Sardis, the capital of the Persian satrapy (498 BC). Although the revolt was eventually suppressed by the Persians in 494 BC, it directly triggered the Greco-Persian Wars and profoundly changed the political landscape of the Eastern Mediterranean.

v.t: Heraclitus proposes the philosophy of 'everything flows'
v.s: The ancient Greek philosopher Heraclitus of Ephesus put forward the core proposition of 'Panta Rhei' (everything flows), believing that all things in the world are in a state of perpetual change. His famous saying 'No man ever steps in the same river twice' reveals the universality of movement and change. Heraclitus also regarded 'fire' as the arche, and 'Logos' as the rational law governing cosmic change. His thought profoundly influenced the development of later dialectics.

v.t: Bantu migration deep into southern Africa
v.s: Over about two millennia, Bantu-speaking peoples spread from west-central Africa to all of southern and eastern Africa. By around 500 BC, Bantu peoples had reached the areas of modern-day Zambia and Zimbabwe, bringing iron smelting, agriculture (sorghum, millet) and herding techniques. The Bantu migration shaped the basic pattern of modern African population and language distribution, and is the most important population migration event in African history.

v.t: Confucius leaves the state of Lu and begins traveling through various states
v.s: Confucius, while serving as Minister of Justice in Lu, had serious disagreements with the ruling Ji Huanzi over governance. Coupled with the machinations of Qi to alienate the Lu court, Confucius felt his ideals could not be realized. He left Lu with disciples including Yan Hui, Zi Lu and Zi Gong, beginning a fourteen-year journey through various states including Wei, Cao, Song, Zheng, Chen, and Cai, spreading Confucian teachings. The traditional date for Confucius leaving Lu is 497 BC, two years later than this entry.

Through a well-organized system of 20 satrapies, uniform weights and measures, common use of Aramaic, and an efficient taxation system, the Persian Empire maintained relative peace over a vast territory from the Indus to the Aegean. 'Pax Persica' secured commercial circulation and cultural exchange within the empire; the western section of the Silk Road began to take shape during this period.

Athens and Eretria responded to the appeal of Miletus, sending 25 ships (20 from Athens, 5 from Eretria) to support the Ionian rebels. This Greek force landed in Asia Minor. The Athenian participation enraged Persian Emperor Darius I, who swore revenge against Athens, becoming a direct trigger for the Greco-Persian Wars. The burning of Sardis occurred in 498 BC, one year later than this entry.

v.t: Sixteen Mahajanapadas (great states) in the Indian 'Age of the Janapadas'
v.s: From the 6th to 4th centuries BC, sixteen major powers (Magadha, Kosala, Vajji, etc.) emerged in the Ganges and Indus river valleys, known as the 'Age of the Janapadas'. Frequent wars for hegemony occurred among these states, while at the same time, the Shramana movements such as Buddhism and Jainism rose, challenging the authority of Brahmanism. Magadha gradually became the most powerful.

v.t: King Helü of Wu attacks Chu and becomes hegemon
v.s: King Helü of Wu, assisted by Wu Zixu and Sun Wu, defeated Chu in the Battle of Boju (506 BC) and captured its capital Ying, nearly destroying Chu. Although Wu later withdrew due to Qin reinforcements and a Yue surprise attack, Wu had replaced Chu as the hegemon of the south. In 498 BC, Wu further consolidated its control over the Jianghuai region, laying the foundation for Fuchai's later northern hegemony.

v.t: Continued struggles for hegemony among Spring and Autumn vassal states
v.s: During the Spring and Autumn period, wars for hegemony among vassal states were frequent, with the four great powers Jin, Chu, Qi, and Qin alternating as hegemons. After the truce mediated by Xiang Xu in 546 BC, warfare decreased relatively, but within each state, the power of ministers and noble families rose, such as the six ministers of Jin monopolizing power and the Tian clan in Qi growing in influence. By the end of the Spring and Autumn period, Wu and Yue in the southeast rose and joined the struggle for hegemony.

v.t: Athens supports Ionian Revolt and burns Sardis
v.s: The Athenian and Eretrian reinforcements, together with the Ionian rebels, attacked and burned Sardis, the capital of the Persian satrapy of Lydia (except the temple of Cybele) in 498 BC. Though they could not hold the city, this action greatly boosted the morale of the rebels and thoroughly enraged Darius I, who resolved to conquer all of Greece.

v.t: Darius I suppresses the Ionian Revolt
v.s: Persian Emperor Darius I mobilized a large army in three columns into Asia Minor to brutally suppress the Ionian Revolt. The Persian army adopted a strategy of divide and conquer, capturing cities while inducing defections from within the rebel ranks. In 497 BC, the Persians captured important cities such as Clazomenae and Cyme, and the revolt turned decisively against the rebels.

The Scythians were a nomadic people active in the northern Black Sea region from the 8th to 3rd centuries BC, known for their exquisite gold work, cavalry tactics, and distinctive 'animal style' art. They built no cities, migrated seasonally, and used wheeled felt tents. Scythian culture deeply influenced ancient Greece, Persia, and later the Sarmatians.

v.t: Persia gradually pacifies the Ionian Revolt
v.s: With naval support, the Persian army attacked each rebel Ionian city-state in turn. In 497 BC, the Persian army captured Canae and Martamya in northern Asia Minor, forcing many smaller city-states to submit again. Divisions emerged among the rebels, and some cities left the alliance and surrendered to Persia.

v.t: Complete failure of the Ionian Revolt
v.s: The Persian navy defeated the Ionian allied fleet at the Battle of Lade, and Miletus was then captured by the Persian army. The rebel leader Aristagoras was killed by Thracians while fleeing. The Persians took brutal revenge on the rebel cities: men were executed, women and children enslaved. After the revolt's failure, Darius I immediately prepared to invade Greece.

After the suppression of the Ionian Revolt, Persian Emperor Darius I used the Athenian and Eretrian aid to the rebels as a pretext to launch a full-scale war against Greece. The Greco-Persian Wars, counted from the Miletus revolt of 499 BC to the Peace of Callias in 449 BC, lasted half a century and included famous battles such as Marathon, Thermopylae, Salamis, and Plataea, ultimately ending in Greek victory.

v.t: Battle of Zuili between Wu and Yue
v.s: King Yunchang of Yue took advantage of King Helü of Wu's expedition against Chu to attack Wu. The two sides fought at Zuili (present-day Jiaxing, Zhejiang). Though not large in scale, this battle marked the beginning of the Wu-Yue rivalry. After Yunchang's death, Goujian succeeded him, and the Wu-Yue conflict further intensified.

v.t: Athenian tyrant Hippias flees to Persia
v.s: Hippias, the former tyrant of Athens expelled in 510 BC, fled to the Persian court in Asia Minor. He actively provided Darius I with military intelligence on Athens and urged the Persians to invade Greece, hoping to restore his own rule in Athens with Persian help.

v.t: Rise of the Five Hegemons of the Spring and Autumn period
v.s: During the Spring and Autumn period, the successive hegemons included Duke Huan of Qi, Duke Wen of Jin, King Zhuang of Chu, King Helü of Wu, and King Goujian of Yue (the 'Five Hegemons'). The hegemon, using the slogan 'respect the king and repel the barbarians', convened alliances of vassals and exercised military authority on behalf of the nominal Zhou king. By 495 BC, Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin had died, King Zhuang of Chu was on the throne, and Wu and Yue were rising.

v.t: Persian Empire prepares for westward expedition against Greece
v.s: After suppressing the Ionian Revolt, Darius I began extensive preparations for a second expedition against Greece (the first in 492 BC having failed). He conscripted troops from across the empire, built transport ships, and stockpiled provisions. The Persian Empire mobilized its vast manpower and resources, aiming to conquer the Greek peninsula in one stroke.

The Athenian politician Themistocles was elected archon. He clearly recognized the threat of the Persian Empire and advocated for the development of a navy. On his initiative, Athens used the proceeds from the Laurium silver mines to build 200 triremes, laying the material foundation for the later victory at Salamis.

v.t: Battle of Lade and fall of Miletus
v.s: The Persian fleet clashed with the Ionian rebel navy near the island of Lade. The rebel navy had about 353 ships, the Persians about 600. Due to the desertion of Samos and other contingents, the rebels were crushed. The Persians then captured Miletus, sacked the city, executed the men, and enslaved the women and children. The civilization of Miletus suffered a devastating blow.

v.t: First secession of the Roman plebs
v.s: The Roman plebs, unable to bear debt bondage and oppression by the patricians, collectively seceded to the Sacred Mount, threatening to build a new city. The patricians were forced to concede, agreeing to establish the office of 'tribune of the plebs' with veto power and inviolability. This was an important victory in the plebeian struggle for rights and promoted the development of the Roman Republic's political system.

v.t: King Fuchai of Wu defeats Yue at the Battle of Fujiao
v.s: King Fuchai of Wu, seeking revenge for his father's death (Helü died from a wound at Zuili in 496 BC), decisively defeated the Yue army at Fujiao (near present-day Lake Tai, Jiangsu). King Goujian of Yue retreated with 5,000 men to Mount Kuaiji and was forced to sue for peace, agreeing to serve as a slave in Wu for three years. Goujian later endured hardships and eventually destroyed Wu in 473 BC.

Themistocles persuaded the Athenian Ecclesia to use the surplus from the Laurium silver mines to build fortifications and ship sheds at Piraeus. Piraeus was developed into Athens' naval base and commercial port, with walls, ship sheds, and warehouses, becoming one of the busiest ports in the Mediterranean.

Rome and the Latin city-states signed the 'Treaty of Cassius', formally establishing the Latin League. The treaty stipulated that Rome and the Latin cities would jointly defend against external enemies, divide spoils equally, and grant mutual rights of intermarriage and trade. This alliance strengthened Rome's leadership in Latium and laid the foundation for its later conquest of Italy.

Persian Emperor Darius I sent his son-in-law Mardonius with a land and sea force to invade Greece. The Persian army subdued Thrace and Macedon, making them Persian vassals. But the navy encountered a storm off Mount Athos, losing over 300 ships and more than 20,000 men. Although the first invasion did not attack mainland Greece, it prepared the ground for a later large-scale invasion.

v.t: Darius I prepares for a second invasion of Greece
v.s: After the failure of the first invasion due to the destruction of his fleet, Darius I did not abandon his plan to conquer Greece. He ordered the construction of more ships and conscripted large numbers of troops and supplies from every satrapy for an even larger expedition. He also sent envoys to Greek city-states demanding 'earth and water' (symbols of submission).

v.t: Persian envoys demand submission of Greek city-states
v.s: Darius I sent envoys to Greek city-states demanding 'earth and water' as tokens of submission. Many city-states complied, but Athens threw the envoys into a pit, and Sparta threw them into a well, telling them to take 'earth and water' from there. This further inflamed the conflict and hardened Persia's resolve to conquer Greece by force.

Persian Emperor Darius I dispatched Datis and Artaphernes with an army of about 25,000 that landed on the plain of Marathon. The Athenian general Miltiades led about 10,000 hoplites and 1,000 Plataean allies. Using the terrain and a clever double-envelopment tactic, the Athenians defeated the Persians, killing about 6,400 while losing only 192. This victory greatly boosted Greek confidence against Persia.

v.t: Pheidippides runs to announce victory (origin of marathon)
v.s: After the Battle of Marathon, the Athenian herald Pheidippides ran about 42 km from Marathon to the Athenian agora, shouted 'We have won!' and collapsed dead. The modern marathon race originates from this legend. In fact, Pheidippides had previously run about 240 km to Sparta to seek aid.

After the defeat at Marathon, the Persian fleet tried to sail around Cape Sounion to attack Athens directly, but the Athenian army quickly returned to defend. Seeing no advantage and having suffered heavy losses, the Persian commander decided to withdraw to Asia. The first large-scale invasion of Greece ended in failure. Darius I died soon after, and his son Xerxes I planned an even larger invasion.

The Athenian general Miltiades led a fleet of 70 ships to attack Paros, seeking revenge for the island supplying ships to the Persians during their invasion. After a siege of over a month, he failed to capture the city and was wounded in the leg. On returning to Athens, he was prosecuted by his political opponents for 'deceiving the people', fined 50 talents, and died soon after of his wound.

v.t: Darius I plans second expedition before his death
v.s: While gravely ill, Persian Emperor Darius I still obsessed over conquering Greece and ordered all satrapies to intensify preparations for an even larger expedition. But before it could be launched, Darius I died at about age 64. His son Xerxes I succeeded him and eventually launched the famous second Persian invasion of Greece.

Building on Cleisthenes' reforms, Athens formally incorporated ostracism into its political system. Each year the Ecclesia voted on whether to hold an ostracism; if so, citizens wrote names on potsherds, and the person with the most votes (provided there were at least 6,000) was exiled for ten years. The system aimed to prevent the return of tyranny. The first person ostracized was Hipparchus in 487 BC.

v.t: King Fuchai of Wu digs the Han Canal
v.s: To facilitate his northern expansion and transport troops and supplies, King Fuchai of Wu ordered the excavation of the Han Canal, connecting the Yangtze and Huai Rivers. The canal ran from Yangzhou northeast to Huai'an, about 150 km long. It was one of the earliest canals in China and later became an important part of the Grand Canal, greatly promoting north-south economic and cultural exchange.

v.t: Darius I dies, Xerxes I succeeds
v.s: Persian Emperor Darius I died while preparing the second Greek expedition. His son Xerxes I succeeded him. Xerxes quickly suppressed an Egyptian revolt (485 BC) and continued his father's plan to invade Greece, eventually leading a huge army (reputed to be a million strong) into Greece in 480 BC.

v.t: Xerxes I suppresses the Egyptian revolt
v.s: After succeeding to the throne, Xerxes I faced a major revolt in the satrapy of Egypt. He led a large army to crush the rebellion with brutal force. He reduced Egypt from a kingdom to a satrapy, appointed no Egyptian governor, and plundered the wealth of Egyptian temples to finance his military campaigns.

v.t: King Goujian of Yue becomes a slave in Wu
v.s: After the defeat at Fujiao, King Goujian of Yue, in accordance with the peace treaty, went to Wu as a slave, accompanied by his wife and minister Fan Li. In Wu, he served Fuchai by tending horses and guarding tombs, enduring humiliation for three years. By bribing the Wu grand tutor Bo Pi, Goujian was eventually released. After returning to Yue, he slept on brushwood and tasted gall, vowing revenge.

v.t: Aeschylus wins first prize at the Dionysia
v.s: The ancient Greek 'Father of Tragedy' Aeschylus won his first victory at the Athenian Dionysia dramatic festival. He wrote about 90 tragedies, of which seven survive, including 'Prometheus Bound' and 'Agamemnon'. He increased the number of actors from one to two and reduced the role of the chorus, laying down the basic form of Greek tragedy.

Themistocles persuaded the Athenian Ecclesia to use the huge revenue from a newly discovered vein at the Laurium silver mines to build 200 triremes, rather than distributing it to citizens. This decision transformed the Athenian navy from a second-rate Mediterranean force into the most powerful fleet, laying the military foundation for the victory at Salamis and the subsequent Athenian Empire.

v.t: Xerxes I assembles a massive army to invade Greece
v.s: After four years of preparation, Persian Emperor Xerxes I gathered a huge army from all satrapies. According to Herodotus, the land army numbered about 1.7 million and the navy over 1,200 ships (modern estimates range from 50,000 to 150,000). The army assembled at Sardis, preparing to cross the Hellespont and invade Greece.

v.t: Goujian returns to Yue and begins 'sleeping on brushwood and tasting gall'
v.s: After being released and returning to his country, King Goujian of Yue vowed revenge. He slept on brushwood and tasted gall each day to remind himself of his humiliation. Goujian adopted Wen Zhong's 'seven methods to defeat Wu' and Fan Li's strategies: building domestic production, training troops, while sending beautiful women (Xi Shi) and treasures to Wu to lull Fuchai.

v.t: Confucius completes the 'Spring and Autumn Annals'
v.s: Based on the chronicles of the state of Lu, Confucius compiled the 'Spring and Autumn Annals', covering 242 years from the first year of Duke Yin of Lu (722 BC) to the fourteenth year of Duke Ai of Lu (481 BC). Using a style of 'subtle and profound words with great meaning', the work praises and condemns historical figures and events. It is the earliest annalistic history of China and one of the Confucian 'Six Classics'. This year, a qilin (mythical beast) was captured, which Confucius took as a bad omen, and he stopped writing.

v.t: Greek city-states form alliance against Persia
v.s: Facing the massive army of Xerxes I, 31 Greek city-states, led by Sparta and Athens, met at Corinth and formed an anti-Persian league. Sparta was the hegemon (due to its strongest land army), and Athens provided the main naval force. The allies agreed to fight together and not to make a separate peace with Persia. This provided organizational basis for the coordinated actions at Thermopylae, Salamis, and other battles.

Persian Emperor Xerxes I invaded Greece with an army of about 300,000. Spartan King Leonidas led about 7,000 Greek allies (including 300 Spartan hoplites) to block the Persian advance at the narrow pass of Thermopylae. After three days of fierce fighting, a local traitor named Ephialtes showed the Persians a mountain path to outflank the Greeks. Leonidas dismissed the allied troops and remained with 300 Spartans and 700 Thespians to cover the retreat; they were all killed. Though a defeat, the battle became a symbol of heroic resistance in Western military history.

After Thermopylae fell, the Persian army marched into Attica. Following Themistocles' plan, the Athenians had evacuated women, children, and elderly to Salamis and Troezen. The Persians entered the empty city of Athens and burned the temples and many buildings on the Acropolis. This atrocity only inflamed the Greeks' determination to fight.

Through Themistocles' stratagem, the Greek allied fleet (about 380 ships) lured the Persian fleet (about 1,000 ships) into the narrow straits of Salamis. The Greeks took advantage of the confined waters to neutralize the Persian numerical advantage, using their more maneuverable triremes to win a decisive victory. The Persians lost about 200 ships, the Greeks only 40. Xerxes I was forced to withdraw to Asia, and the war turned in Greece's favor.

v.t: Queen Artemisia participates in the Battle of Salamis
v.s: Artemisia, queen of Caria (ruler of Halicarnassus), as an ally of the Persian Empire, personally led five ships at the Battle of Salamis. She fought bravely and, when retreating, cleverly rammed a friendly ship to evade pursuit, earning Xerxes' admiration. Herodotus recorded her deeds in detail in his 'Histories'.

The Greek allied army of about 40,000 (mainly Spartan) under Spartan regent Pausanias fought a decisive land battle against the Persian army of about 70,000 under Mardonius at Plataea. The Greek hoplite phalanx broke the Persian infantry; Mardonius was killed. This victory completely crushed the Persian land invasion, marking a decisive Greek triumph in the Greco-Persian Wars.

On the same day (or a few days later), the Greek fleet defeated the remaining Persian navy at Cape Mycale. The Greek marines landed and routed the Persian army. The Greek cities of Asia Minor again revolted and broke free from Persian rule. This battle marked the complete loss of Persian naval supremacy.

Confucius died in the state of Lu at the age of 73. In his later years, Confucius returned to Lu and devoted himself to editing the ancient classics: the 'Book of Songs', 'Book of Documents', 'Book of Rites', 'Book of Music', 'I Ching', and 'Spring and Autumn Annals'. His disciples and their followers compiled his sayings into the 'Analects'. Confucian thought became the mainstream of Chinese traditional culture for over two thousand years, influencing East Asia and the world.

To continue the fight against Persia and liberate the Greek cities of Asia Minor, Athens and the Aegean island and Ionian coastal cities formed an alliance on the island of Delos, known as the Delian League. Each member was required to contribute either ships or money (tribute). The treasury was on Delos. With its powerful navy, Athens gradually became the leader of the league, eventually turning it into the Athenian Empire.

v.t: Goujian implements his revenge plan after returning to Yue
v.s: After returning to Yue, King Goujian adopted Wen Zhong's 'nine methods to defeat Wu', including sending beautiful women (Xi Shi, Zheng Dan) and large amounts of timber to Wu to exhaust its resources, while at home encouraging childbirth, developing ironworking, and training troops. Goujian personally took part in farming, and his wife wove cloth, sharing hardships with the people; national strength recovered rapidly.

v.t: Tensions rise between Athens and Sparta
v.s: After the Greco-Persian Wars, Athens' leadership in the Delian League grew increasingly strong, while Sparta maintained its hegemony over the Peloponnesian League. Clashes over spheres of influence occurred on the Greek mainland. Sparta protested against Athens building the Long Walls (connecting Athens to Piraeus), but Themistocles' diplomacy allowed Athens to complete them.

v.t: King Yuan of Zhou ascends the throne
v.s: King Jing of Zhou died, and his son Ji Ren succeeded him as King Yuan of Zhou. During King Yuan's reign (476-469 BC), the Spring and Autumn period drew to a close, and the trend of the three clans dividing Jin became clear. The Zhou royal house weakened further, its status as nominal 'Son of Heaven' existing in name only.

The Athenian general Cimon led the Delian League fleet on an expedition to the northern Aegean, capturing Scyros, Carystus, and other Persian strongholds, driving Persian forces from the Thracian coast. Cimon brought back the 'bones of Theseus' from Scyros, greatly inspiring Athenian patriotism. His military victories expanded Athens' naval influence and laid the foundation for Athenian hegemony over the Aegean.

The ancient Greek philosopher Parmenides of the Eleatic School, in his philosophical poem 'On Nature', put forward the ontological proposition: 'Being is, non-being is not.' In direct opposition to Heraclitus' doctrine of flux, Parmenides argued that true reality is eternal, unchanging, indivisible 'Being', and that change is mere illusion of the senses. Parmenides inaugurated the tradition of logical argumentation in Western philosophy, profoundly influencing Plato and later metaphysics.

v.t: Hiero I, tyrant of Syracuse, reigns
v.s: During the reign of Hiero I (478-467 BC), tyrant of Syracuse in Sicily, Syracuse became the most powerful Greek city-state in the west. He defeated the Etruscans at the Battle of Cumae in 474 BC, removing the Etruscan threat to the Greek cities of southern Italy and gaining control of the Tyrrhenian Sea. Hiero I was also a patron of culture; poets Pindar and Aeschylus visited his court.

After 'ten years of growth and ten years of training', King Goujian of Yue took advantage of King Fuchai of Wu's absence at a conference in Huangchi to invade Wu in three columns. In 473 BC, the Yue army captured the Wu capital Gusu; Fuchai was besieged on Mount Gusu, and after failing to negotiate surrender, he committed suicide. Having destroyed Wu, Goujian marched north and convened an alliance of vassal states at Xuzhou, becoming the last hegemon of the Spring and Autumn period.

Athens used ostracism to expel Themistocles. Reasons for his exile included his radical anti-Spartan policies, suspicion of secret dealings with Persia (later proven false), and Athenian fear of his accumulating power. Themistocles first fled to Argos, then to Persia. His departure allowed Cimon's faction to dominate Athens.

After Themistocles was ostracized, Cimon became the most influential politician and military commander in Athens. He pursued a pro-Spartan foreign policy while actively leading the Delian League in operations against Persia. This year, Cimon led the fleet to clear remaining Persian strongholds in the Aegean, further consolidating Athens' naval hegemony.

Pausanias, the hero of Plataea and regent of Sparta, was sentenced to death by the Spartan ephors and council of elders on suspicion of collaborating with Persia and plotting to become tyrant of Greece. He took refuge in a temple but was walled in and starved to death (or, according to another account, suffocated). This incident reflects Sparta's deep suspicion of individual ambition.

The great ancient Greek philosopher Socrates was born in Athens, the son of a stonemason. He developed the Socratic method (elenchus) – a form of cooperative argumentative dialogue to stimulate critical thinking and illuminate ideas. His famous maxim 'Know thyself' remains influential. Socrates wrote nothing; his teachings and methods are preserved through the writings of his student Plato, profoundly shaping later Western philosophy.

v.t: Sophocles defeats Aeschylus in the dramatic competition for the first time
v.s: The young Sophocles won first prize at the Athenian Dionysia, defeating Aeschylus. Sophocles reformed drama: increasing the number of actors to three, strengthening character development, and reducing the role of the chorus. He wrote 123 plays, of which seven survive, including 'Antigone' and 'Oedipus Rex'.

After its disastrous defeat in the Greco-Persian Wars and facing internal revolts in Egypt and Babylon, the Persian Empire lacked the strength to mount another large-scale invasion of Greece. After Xerxes I, Persia's strategic focus shifted to internal stability and dealing with rebellions; it essentially abandoned expansion into Europe and instead adopted a policy of supporting rival Greek city-states to maintain a balance of power.

The Athenian general Cimon led the Delian League fleet to a decisive victory over the Persian land and naval forces at the mouth of the Eurymedon River in southern Asia Minor. The Persians lost about 200 ships and many infantry. This battle completely destroyed the Persian navy's fighting capacity, and control of the Aegean passed irrevocably to Athens. Persia was forced to agree to a truce, ceasing its attacks on the Greek cities of Asia Minor.

Persian Emperor Xerxes I was assassinated in a palace coup by his grand vizier Artabanus. Artabanus then tried to place Xerxes' third son Artaxerxes I on the throne but was discovered. Xerxes' son Artaxerxes I (Artaxerxes I) suppressed the rebellion and succeeded as emperor. This internal strife weakened the central authority of the Persian Empire.

Despite the internal turmoil in Persia, the Delian League did not cease its military actions. Cimon led the fleet in operations near Cyprus, attempting to expel Persian forces entirely from the eastern Mediterranean. The Delian League reached the height of its power, and Athens became undisputed master of the Aegean.

v.t: Great earthquake in Sparta and Helot revolt
v.s: A powerful earthquake struck Sparta, causing massive casualties and nearly leveling the city. The Helots and other subjected peoples took advantage of the disaster to launch a large-scale revolt. The Spartans, facing an existential crisis, sought help from Athens. Cimon led an Athenian force to aid Sparta, but the Spartans, fearing the influence of Athenian democratic ideas on their own system, eventually dismissed the Athenian troops, angering the Athenians.

Inaros, a Libyan prince, launched a major revolt in the Nile Delta against Persian rule. Inaros received support from Athens, which sent 200 ships (some say 40) to Egypt. The rebels controlled all of Lower Egypt for a time, but were finally crushed by Persian forces in 454 BC.

v.t: Ephialtes' reforms – deepening of Athenian democracy
v.s: Ephialtes, the leader of the Athenian democratic faction, with the support of Pericles, pushed through a law that stripped the Areopagus (Council of the Areopagus) of its political powers (review of officials' qualifications, oversight of laws), leaving it only the judicial function of trying homicide cases. These powers were transferred to the Council of Five Hundred, the Ecclesia, and the popular courts. This was the most important advancement of Athenian democracy since Cleisthenes' reforms.

Ephialtes, the Athenian democratic reformer, was assassinated by hired agents of the opposition not long after successfully curbing the powers of the Areopagus. His death made Pericles the leader of the Athenian democratic faction. Pericles continued to advance democratization, including introducing state pay for public office and lowering property qualifications, eventually bringing Athenian democracy to its peak.

Athens used ostracism to expel the pro-Spartan Cimon for ten years. Cimon's exile marked the complete defeat of the conservative faction by the democratic faction (led by Ephialtes and Pericles). This cleared the political obstacles for further democratic reforms and shifted Athenian foreign policy toward confrontation with Sparta.

Athens came into conflict with Sparta's allies Corinth and Epidaurus. Sparta marched into Attica, and the First Peloponnesian War (460-445 BC) began. Athens allied with Argos and Thessaly against the Spartan alliance. The war was fought intermittently and ended with the Thirty Years' Peace.

The ancient Greek physician Hippocrates, later revered as the 'Father of Medicine', was born on the island of Cos. He proposed the humoral theory (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile), arguing that disease resulted from imbalance of humors rather than divine intervention. The Hippocratic Oath, which he established as an ethical code for physicians, remains a professional standard worldwide.

From 460 to 429 BC, Pericles was elected strategos (general) fifteen consecutive times, ushering in the Golden Age of Athens. During his tenure, he advanced democratic reforms: opening all public offices to all citizens, introducing state pay for public service so even the poor could participate, and strengthening the legislative power of the Ecclesia. Under Pericles, Athens rebuilt the Acropolis, which had been burned by the Persians, and constructed the Parthenon and other immortal buildings. Athens became the cultural center and leader of the Greek world. Pericles' Funeral Oration became a classic statement of democratic ideals.

After the assassination of Ephialtes, Pericles became the leader of the Athenian democratic faction. He further reformed the system: raising the daily pay for jurors from 2 obols to 3, enabling poor citizens to participate in the judiciary, and passing a law allowing the third class (zeugitai) to serve as archons. Athenian democracy reached its zenith.

The ancient Greek tragedian Aeschylus' final work, the 'Oresteia' trilogy ('Agamemnon', 'The Libation Bearers', 'The Eumenides'), was performed at the Athenian Dionysia and won first prize. It is the only complete extant trilogy from Western drama, recounting the end of the curse on the house of Atreus and the origin of the Areopagus court.

A Spartan army marched north to support Thebes and met an Athenian army at Tanagra. The Athenians, with Cimon (who had been recalled early) in command, although they did not win, halted the Spartan advance. After this battle, Athens quickly rebuilt and consolidated its democratic government and built the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus.

After the Battle of Tanagra, the Athenians recognized the importance of walls for defense against Spartan land forces. Under Pericles' leadership, Athens built the Long Walls from the city to the port of Piraeus, about 6 km long. The Long Walls ensured that even if Attica was invaded and the land cut off, Athens could still be supplied by sea.

The ancient Greek 'Father of Tragedy' Aeschylus died in Gela, Sicily, at about age 69. According to legend, he was killed when an eagle dropped a tortoise on his bald head. His epitaph did not mention his dramatic achievements but only his military service at Marathon. Aeschylus' contributions to drama included adding a second actor, reducing the chorus, using elaborate language and stage settings.

The Athenian fleet sent to support Inaros' revolt in Egypt was besieged by Persian forces on the island of Prosopitis in the Nile Delta for 18 months. The Persians diverted the river, leaving the Athenian ships aground, and then destroyed them. Only a few survivors escaped through Libya to Athens. This disaster severely weakened Athenian power and forced Athens to make peace with Persia.

v.t: Delian League treasury moved from Delos to Athens
v.s: Under the pretext that Delos was insecure (or to prevent a Persian attack), Athens moved the Delian League's treasury from Delos to the Acropolis in Athens. From then on, the allies' funds were completely controlled by Athens, which could use the tribute to build the Parthenon and maintain a powerful navy. The Delian League in effect became the Athenian Empire; military operations were directed by Athens.

v.t: Athens and Persia sign a five-year truce
v.s: Due to the disaster in Egypt and the Spartan threat, Athens and the Persian Empire reached a preliminary truce, agreeing not to attack each other for five years. This gave Athens a breathing space to focus on the pressure from the Peloponnesian War. Persia used the time to recover and observe the Greek situation for later intervention.

v.t: Rome establishes the Decemviri (Ten Men) lawmaking commission
v.s: In 452 BC, the conflict between plebeians and patricians in the Roman Republic entered a new phase. At the instigation of the tribune Terentilius and others, the Senate agreed to send an embassy to Athens to study the laws of Solon. In 451 BC, the Decemviri (Ten Men) were established, five patricians and five plebeians, with power to suspend all magistracies and codify laws. The Decemviri issued the Law of the Twelve Tables in 449-450 BC, which became the foundation of Roman law.

v.t: Law of the Twelve Tables promulgated in Rome
v.s: The Roman Decemviri completed the 'Law of the Twelve Tables', inscribed on bronze tablets and displayed publicly. The law covered summons, trial, debt, paternal power, inheritance, ownership, tort, criminal law, and religious law, limiting the patricians' arbitrary interpretation of law. Although the provisions were still unfavorable to the plebeians (e.g., forbidding intermarriage between patricians and plebeians), it was the starting point for the development of Roman law.

The Athenian general Cimon led a fleet to Cyprus and besieged the Persian stronghold of Citium, where he died of illness (some sources say in battle). On his deathbed, he ordered the fleet to withdraw immediately to avoid defeat. Cimon was the leader of the Athenian aristocratic party and a great commander whose victories, such as the Battle of the Eurymedon, made Athens the dominant sea power.

The ancient Greek writer Herodotus, later called the 'Father of History', completed his great work 'The Histories' (also known as 'The Greco-Persian Wars'). In nine books, it recounts the wars between Greece and Persia, and in the process describes the geography, customs, and history of Greece, Persia, Egypt, and Babylon. He founded the tradition of historical writing in the West, emphasizing gathering evidence, verification, and causal explanation.

v.t: Hanno the Carthaginian sails to West Africa
v.s: Hanno, a Carthaginian admiral, led a fleet of 60 ships and about 30,000 people from Carthage south along the west coast of Africa. He established several colonies (such as Cerne) and may have reached as far as modern-day Sierra Leone or Cameroon. Hanno's voyage report, inscribed on a tablet and preserved in a temple, was a remarkable feat of ancient African navigation.

v.t: Nazca Lines begin to be drawn
v.s: Starting around 500 BC, the indigenous people of the Nazca Desert in southern Peru began to draw huge lines and geometric shapes on the earth's surface, creating the world-famous Nazca Lines. The figures include hummingbirds, spiders, monkeys, and other giant animal shapes that can only be fully seen from the air. Their purpose may have been astronomical, religious, or for water rituals; they remain a mystery.

v.t: Peace of Callias – end of the Greco-Persian Wars
v.s: The Athenian envoy Callias concluded a peace treaty with Persian Emperor Artaxerxes I, ending the half-century of Greco-Persian Wars. The treaty stipulated that Persia recognized the independence of the Greek cities of Asia Minor; Persian warships would not enter the Aegean; and Persia gave up control of the Hellespont and Bosporus. Athens gained naval supremacy in the eastern Mediterranean, while Persia focused on internal affairs.

v.t: Mayan city-states enter the Classic period
v.s: Mayan civilization in Mesoamerica entered its Preclassic period around 250 BC, but some important cities such as Kaminaljuyú flourished as early as this time. The Maya developed hieroglyphic writing, complex calendar systems, and large pyramid architecture. The Classic period (AD 250-900) saw the peak of Mayan civilization.

Pericles proposed a Panhellenic congress to discuss rebuilding the temples destroyed by the Persians, ensuring maritime safety, and achieving a lasting peace in Greece. But Sparta refused to attend, and the congress came to nothing. This reflected the growing rift between Athens and Sparta, which eventually led to the Peloponnesian War.

v.t: Construction of the Parthenon begins on the Acropolis
v.s: Under Pericles' leadership, Athens began building the Parthenon on the Acropolis as a temple to the city's patron goddess Athena. The architects Ictinus and Callicrates designed it, using white marble throughout, and the sculptor Phidias oversaw the decorative program. The Parthenon is the pinnacle of ancient Greek architecture; its use of Doric columns and golden ratios has influenced Western architecture for over two millennia.

The Athenian army was defeated by Thebes and the Boeotian League at Coronea in Boeotia. Athens lost control of Boeotia and was forced to recognize the independence of the Boeotian cities. This defeat reduced Athenian influence in central Greece and prompted Athens to seek a temporary peace with Sparta.

v.t: Thirty Years' Peace between Athens and Sparta
v.s: Athens and Sparta signed the 'Thirty Years' Peace'. The treaty stipulated that each side would maintain its own alliance system (the Delian League for Athens, the Peloponnesian League for Sparta) and not interfere in the other's internal affairs; disputes would be settled by arbitration; and neither side would poach the other's allies. The peace lasted 14 years before breaking down over the Corinth-Corcyra conflict.

Pericles pushed through a citizenship law that restricted Athenian citizenship to those born of two Athenian parents. The law aimed to maintain the purity and welfare benefits (such as state pay and grain distributions) of the citizen body, but it disenfranchised many metics (resident foreigners) and children of mixed marriages. Athenian democracy became more exclusive.

v.t: Phidias completes the Athena Parthenos statue
v.s: The renowned sculptor Phidias completed the colossal gold-and-ivory statue of Athena Parthenos (Athena the Virgin) inside the Parthenon. The statue stood about 12 meters high and was one of the most famous sculptural works of ancient Greece. It symbolized Athens' wealth and artistic achievement, representing the peak of classical Greek art.

The ancient Greek philosopher Democritus was active in Athens. He was the main proponent of atomism, believing that the world consists of indivisible 'atoms' and 'void', and that the generation and destruction of all things are merely the combination and separation of atoms. He wrote extensively on philosophy, physics, mathematics, ethics, etc., and his thought had a great influence on later philosophy and science.

The Athenian Ecclesia again elected Pericles as strategos (general). From his first term in 460 BC until his death in 429 BC, he was elected almost every year (missing only one). Pericles, holding the highest military and administrative office continuously, became the de facto leader of Athens, but his power was always constrained by the democratic system.

Sophocles, one of the three great Athenian tragedians, first performed his masterpiece 'Antigone' at the Dionysia. The play tells the story of Antigone, the daughter of Oedipus, who defies King Creon's decree to bury her brother Polynices, leading to her tragic death. It explores the conflict between state law and moral conscience, secular power and divine law.

Samos, a member of the Delian League, quarreled with Miletus over territory. Athens intervened on the side of Miletus, and Samos seceded from the league. Pericles personally led a fleet to capture Samos, dissolved its democratic government, confiscated its fleet, and demanded a large indemnity. Samos was the last ally to dare challenge Athenian hegemony; thereafter the Delian League became fully the Athenian Empire.

After a nine-month siege, Samos surrendered. Pericles ordered the demolition of Samos' walls, confiscated its entire navy, and demanded a heavy indemnity. This suppression further centralized the Athenian Empire; the autonomy of member states was eliminated, and tribute and military contributions were unilaterally decided by Athens.

v.t: Roman legal development after the Twelve Tables
v.s: Building on the Twelve Tables, Rome passed the Lex Canuleia, allowing intermarriage between patricians and plebeians, and the Lex Aquilia regulating damages to property. These legal adjustments eased the conflict between patricians and plebeians and laid the foundation for the stability of the Roman Republic.

Phidias completed all the sculptural decoration of the Parthenon, including the pediment reliefs, the frieze of the Panathenaic procession, and the colossal gold-and-ivory statue of Athena. These sculptures represent the highest level of classical Greek art; their proportions, drapery, and movement became models for later Western sculpture.

Athens sent about 10,000 colonists to found the city of Amphipolis on the Strymon River. The site was near the gold and silver mines of Mount Pangaion and controlled the important trade route to Thrace and the Black Sea. Amphipolis became an important economic pillar and strategic base for the Athenian Empire, changing hands several times during the Peloponnesian War.

Civil strife broke out in Epidamnus, a colony of Corinth, and appealed to its mother city Corinth for aid. But Corcyra (another colony of Corinth) also claimed protective rights over Epidamnus. The two sides clashed, and Corcyra defeated the Corinthian fleet. Fearing retaliation, Corcyra asked Athens for help, and Athens eventually accepted Corcyra into the Delian League. This directly triggered the Peloponnesian War.

After a heated debate in the Athenian Ecclesia, on Pericles' advice, Athens decided to form a 'defensive alliance' with Corcyra – promising aid only if Corcyra was attacked by Corinth, not a full military alliance. This disrupted the stability of the Peloponnesian League, and Corinth, feeling betrayed, pressured Sparta to declare war on Athens.

Athens, under Pericles, issued a harsh economic sanction against Megara, an ally of Corinth: banning Megarian ships from using any harbor or market in the Athenian Empire, and barring Megarians from the Acropolis and the Agora. The decree aimed to cripple Megara's economy and demonstrate Athens' naval hegemony, and was one of the direct triggers of the Peloponnesian War.

Potidaea, a Corinthian colony on the Chalcidice peninsula, was also a member of the Delian League. Fearing that Potidaea might collude with Corinth, Athens ordered it to tear down its seaward walls, give hostages, and dismiss Corinthian officials. Potidaea refused and declared its secession from the Delian League; Corinth sent troops to support it. Athens besieged Potidaea, escalating the conflict.

v.t: Athens and Corcyra conclude formal alliance
v.s: Athens and Corcyra signed a formal military alliance, agreeing not to attack each other and to defend each other. This effectively brought the Corcyraean navy (the third largest in Greece) into the Athenian camp, further irritating Corinth and Sparta, making war inevitable.

v.t: Peloponnesian League issues ultimatum to Athens
v.s: Sparta convened a congress of the Peloponnesian League. Corinth, Megara, and other states denounced Athens' aggression. The congress voted by majority to declare war on Athens and issued an ultimatum: Athens must raise the siege of Potidaea, revoke the Megarian Decree, and allow all Greek states to be autonomous. Athens refused, and war was imminent.

The Athenian Ecclesia voted overwhelmingly to reaffirm the Megarian Decree. In his speech, Pericles insisted that Athens had the right to maintain its empire and would never yield to Spartan threats. This decision extinguished the last hope for a diplomatic solution, and the Peloponnesian War was about to break out in full.

Thebes attacked Plataea, an Athenian ally, by night, and Sparta led the Peloponnesian army into Attica. The Peloponnesian War had officially begun. The underlying cause was the growth of the Athenian Empire, which aroused fear in Sparta, Corinth and other states. The war lasted 27 years (431-404 BC), engulfed the entire Greek world, and ended in Athenian defeat.

In winter, Pericles gave his famous funeral oration over the war dead. He praised Athenian democracy: 'Its administration favors the many instead of the few; this is why it is called a democracy. ... The freedom we enjoy in our government extends also to our ordinary life.' Thucydides recorded the speech in his 'History of the Peloponnesian War', making it a classic document in the history of Western political thought.

Athens and Sparta, two Greek alliances, went to war for hegemony over Greece, a conflict known as the Peloponnesian War. The war lasted 27 years (431-404 BC), and Thucydides recorded it in detail in his 'History of the Peloponnesian War'. In the end, Sparta, with Persian support, won, but the whole Greek world was greatly weakened, paving the way for the rise of Macedon.

A devastating plague broke out in Athens. It is believed to have entered through the port of Piraeus from Egypt. Symptoms included high fever, violent coughing, and skin sores; the mortality rate was extremely high. About a quarter of Athens' population died, including Pericles. The plague severely weakened Athens' war capability and was a major reason for Athens' defeat in the Peloponnesian War. Modern scholars speculate it may have been typhus, plague, or Ebola.

v.t: Pericles removed from office and fined
v.s: Due to the plague and military setbacks, the Athenian Ecclesia, incited by political opponents, removed Pericles from the generalship and fined him (about 15-50 talents). This reflects the feature of Athenian democracy where leaders were subject to popular oversight and accountability. But soon the Athenians regretted it and re-elected Pericles as general.

Pericles contracted the plague during the great Athenian plague and died in the autumn of that year, at about age 66. His death marked the end of the Golden Age of Athens. After him, no comparable statesman emerged to unite the democratic and conservative factions, and Athens' leadership in the Peloponnesian War became reckless and fragmented.

Spartan King Archidamus II, after a siege of two years, captured Plataea. Plataea was a loyal ally of Athens in Boeotia. The Spartans destroyed the city, executed about 200 defenders, and sold the women and children into slavery. Athens lost its foothold in central Greece.

After a two-year siege, Potidaea, starved and without relief, surrendered to Athens. Athens expelled all Corinthian citizens from the city, demolished its walls, and confiscated its property. The siege of Potidaea was extremely costly and further drained Athens' resources.

Mytilene on the island of Lesbos, dissatisfied with the imperial rule of Athens, united with other cities on the island to declare withdrawal from the Delian League and appealed to Sparta for help. Athens sent forces to besiege Mytilene. This revolt posed a serious challenge to Athens' alliance system.

v.t: Political divisions in Athens after Pericles
v.s: After Pericles' death, Athenian politics split into two factions: the radical democrats led by Cleon, who advocated a hard line against allies and Sparta, and the conservatives led by Nicias, who favored truce and negotiation. This internal division led to erratic policies and laid the groundwork for the disaster of the Sicilian Expedition.

Sparta completely destroyed Plataea and annexed its territory to Thebes. The survivors of Plataea fled to Athens and were granted Athenian citizenship. The destruction of Plataea became a symbol of Greek sympathy for Athens and hatred of Sparta.

The Athenian Ecclesia debated whether to execute all the men of Mytilene for their revolt. Cleon argued for massacre to deter other allies; Diodotus argued for executing only the ringleaders to win goodwill. The assembly first passed the massacre resolution, but the next day narrowly reversed it. Athens quickly sent a trireme to countermand the order, and in the end only about 1,000 ringleaders were executed, and the walls were demolished. Thucydides recorded this famous debate.

The ancient Greek philosopher Plato was born in Athens to an aristocratic family. His original name was Aristocles; he was nicknamed 'Plato' (broad) because of his robust physique. At age twenty he became a student of Socrates. After Socrates' death, he traveled to Megara, Sicily, and Egypt. At around forty, he returned to Athens and founded the Academy. Plato wrote over thirty dialogues, proposing the theory of Forms, the doctrine of recollection, and the ideal state. His philosophical system had a foundational impact on Western metaphysics, epistemology, and political philosophy.

The Athenian general Demosthenes led forces into Aetolia in an attempt to gain control of central-western Greece. Due to overconfidence and difficult terrain, the Athenians were defeated by local light troops, suffering heavy losses. Demosthenes withdrew and learned from his mistakes, later achieving a great victory at Pylos.

The Athenian general Demosthenes established a fort at Pylos on the southwest coast of the Peloponnese. A Spartan force attacked, and a contingent of Spartan hoplites became trapped on the island of Sphacteria. After a standoff of several months, the Athenians, using light troops and fire, forced 292 Spartans to surrender, including 120 full Spartan citizens. This was a major blow to Sparta, and Athenian morale soared.

After the battle of Sphacteria, Athens controlled the Pylos peninsula and garrisoned it with Messenian light troops. These Messenians raided Spartan territory and incited Helots to flee, seriously destabilizing Spartan society. Sparta was forced to sue for peace, but Athens refused.

Athens fought the Boeotian League at Delium in Boeotia. The Athenian army was defeated, losing about 1,000 men. This defeat forced Athens to withdraw its garrisons from Boeotia and lose its military influence there. The philosopher Socrates, serving as a hoplite, participated in the battle and was praised for his bravery in retreat.

v.t: Spartan general Brasidas campaigns in Thrace
v.s: The brilliant Spartan general Brasidas led a small elite force north through Thessaly into Macedon and Thrace. Using a combination of diplomacy and military skill, he persuaded several important Athenian allies (such as Amphipolis and Acanthus) to defect from the Delian League. This action severely damaged Athens' economic lifeline and grain supply route.

The Athenian general and historian Thucydides was sentenced by the Athenian Ecclesia to 20 years of exile for failing to relieve Amphipolis in time from Brasidas. During his exile, he traveled extensively, interviewed eyewitnesses of the war, and wrote his 'History of the Peloponnesian War'.

v.t: Athens and Sparta agree to a one-year truce
v.s: To exchange prisoners and gain time, Athens and Sparta signed a one-year truce. But Brasidas continued his operations in Thrace, ignoring the truce, and the armistice broke down.

The Athenian general Cleon led a force to attack Amphipolis and engaged the Spartan general Brasidas. Both generals led from the front; Cleon was killed, and Brasidas died of his wounds. The Athenian army was routed, and Thrace fell completely into Spartan hands. The death of the war-hawk Cleon created conditions for peace negotiations.

Athens and Sparta signed the 'Peace of Nicias', which provided for returning to pre-war conditions, exchanging prisoners and occupied territories, and was supposed to last fifty years. But the peace was not stable; Peloponnesian League members Corinth and Thebes refused to recognize it. The war was effectively only suspended for six years, as Athens launched the Sicilian Expedition in 415 BC.

The young and charismatic Athenian politician Alcibiades emerged after the Peace of Nicias. He was born into an aristocratic family, was the ward of Pericles, and a student and close friend of Socrates. Alcibiades advocated an expansionist policy, opposing peaceful coexistence with Sparta. He successfully allied Athens with Argos and other anti-Spartan states in the Peloponnese, seeking to break Spartan hegemony.

The Moche culture (c. 1-700 AD) emerged on the northern coast of Peru. The Moche are known for their fine metalwork, pottery, and large pyramid architecture (such as the Huaca del Sol and Huaca de la Luna). They developed complex irrigation systems, grew corn, beans and cotton, and formed a hierarchical society of priest-warriors.

v.t: Preparation for the Athenian Sicilian Expedition
v.s: Segesta in Sicily asked Athens for aid against Syracuse. Alcibiades proposed to the Ecclesia an expedition to Sicily, aiming to conquer Syracuse, control Sicily, and thereby gain immense wealth and strategic advantage. Nicias strongly opposed it, but the people, fired up by Alcibiades, approved the expedition.

Spartan King Agis II led an army against a coalition of Argos, Athens, Mantinea, and others at Mantinea. This was the largest land battle of the Peloponnesian War up to that point. The Spartan hoplites, with discipline and tactics, defeated the coalition, re-establishing Sparta's hegemony in the Peloponnese.

v.t: Last large-scale use of ostracism in Athens
v.s: Athens held an ostracism vote, and Alcibiades, in alliance with other factions, had his political opponent Hyperbolus (a manipulative democratic politician) exiled. This was the last major use of ostracism in Athens; the system was gradually abandoned thereafter. Hyperbolus became the only commoner (rather than aristocrat or leader) to be ostracized in Athenian history.

An Athenian fleet attacked the neutral city of Melos, a Spartan colony. Athenian envoys negotiated with the Melian rulers, demanding unconditional surrender and membership in the Delian League. Thucydides recorded the dialogue in his history, and the debate between justice and power became a classic text of international political realism. Melos refused, and after its fall the Athenians killed all adult men and enslaved the women and children.

Just before the Athenian Sicilian expedition fleet sailed, the mutilation of the Hermae statues occurred in Athens. Alcibiades' political enemies accused him of involvement. Alcibiades demanded an immediate trial, but after the fleet sailed he was recalled to face charges. On his way back, Alcibiades fled to Sparta, revealing Athens' military plans to the Spartans and advising them to aid Syracuse.

Athens sent a massive expeditionary force (134 triremes, about 5,100 hoplites, 1,300 light infantry, and many support personnel, about 25,000 men) under the joint command of Nicias, Lamachus, and Alcibiades (the latter absent). It was the most expensive military operation Athens ever undertook, aimed at conquering Syracuse.

The Athenian army under Nicias laid siege to Syracuse. They built a circular wall to blockade the city by land and used their navy to block the harbor. But Syracuse, aided by Spartan reinforcements under Gylippus and Corinthians, adopted counter-siege tactics, constantly harassing the Athenians. The situation became a stalemate.

On Alcibiades' advice, the Spartan army captured and permanently garrisoned Decelea in northern Attica. This prevented Athens from using the silver mines and farmland of that region, and about 20,000 slaves escaped. Athens became blockaded by land and dependent on sea supply.

v.t: Complete destruction of the Athenian Sicilian Expedition
v.s: The Syracusan navy defeated the Athenian fleet in the Great Harbor, closing the escape route. The Athenian army tried to retreat inland but was intercepted by combined Syracusan and Spartan forces. Nicias and Demosthenes were captured and executed; about 7,000 Athenian soldiers were captured and imprisoned in quarries, most dying of disease and starvation. Athens lost about 200 ships, 50,000 men, and enormous wealth, dealing a devastating blow to its war capability.

Sparta signed treaties with the Persian satraps Tissaphernes and Pharnabazus in Asia Minor, stipulating that Sparta recognized Persian sovereignty over Asia Minor and Cyprus, and Persia provided funds for Sparta to build a fleet. Persia used its gold to support Sparta, aiming to weaken Athens and recover control of the Greek cities of Asia Minor.

After the Sicilian disaster, Athens was financially exhausted and morale low; the democratic system came under challenge. Some aristocratic secret societies plotted to overthrow democracy and establish an oligarchy.

v.t: Athenian coup of the Four Hundred
v.s: A coup took place in Athens, and the 'Council of the Four Hundred' came to power, abolishing democracy and depriving the Ecclesia and popular courts of their powers, limiting political rights to 5,000 of the wealthiest citizens. But the Athenian fleet stationed on Samos remained loyal to democracy and recalled Alcibiades as commander. Four months later, the democrats overthrew the oligarchs and restored democracy, but limited it to the '5,000'.

v.t: Athenian fleet restores democracy on Samos
v.s: The sailors of the Athenian fleet on Samos held their own assembly, declared their loyalty to democracy, and elected Alcibiades as general. Alcibiades led the fleet against the Peloponnesian fleet and won several victories, improving Athens' military situation.

The Athenian navy under Alcibiades defeated the Peloponnesian fleet at the Battle of Cyzicus. The Athenians destroyed or captured about 60 enemy ships; the Spartan admiral Mindarus was killed. After this battle, Athens regained control of the Hellespont, cutting off Sparta's access to the Black Sea grain route.

After the victory at Cyzicus, the Athenian democrats fully overthrew the oligarchic regime of the Four Hundred, restoring the Ecclesia, popular courts, and the lot system for all public offices, removing property qualifications. Athenian democracy, after a short regression, returned to the level of Pericles' time.

Carthage, taking advantage of the weakening of the Greek world due to the Peloponnesian War, invaded Sicily. The Carthaginian army captured Greek cities such as Himera and massacred many Greek residents. This invasion began a decades-long struggle for Sicily between Carthage and Syracuse.

After his victories at Cyzicus and elsewhere, Alcibiades led his fleet back to Athens. He was received as a hero; the Ecclesia dropped all charges against him, restored his property and citizenship, and elected him supreme commander. But his prestige had diminished, and some Athenians remained suspicious.

Sparta appointed Lysander as its naval commander. Lysander was shrewd and capable; he forged a close relationship with the Persian prince Cyrus the Younger, securing substantial financial support, allowing him to pay Sparta's rowers triple the Athenian rate, attracting many Athenian mercenaries to defect. He rebuilt the Spartan fleet and adopted new tactics.

The Athenian navy defeated the Spartan fleet at the Arginusae Islands, sinking about 70 enemy ships; the Spartan admiral Callicratidas was killed. But due to a storm, the Athenian fleet failed to rescue the survivors of their own disabled ships (about 1,000 men). The Athenian Ecclesia tried and condemned eight generals (including six who had actually fought) to death. This was a notorious instance of democratic Athens killing its best commanders, leading to a loss of capable military leadership.

The ancient Greek tragedian Euripides died in Macedon at about age 74. He wrote about 92 plays, of which 18 survive, including 'Medea', 'Hippolytus', 'The Trojan Women', and 'The Bacchae'. Euripides' dramas focused more on the psychology of ordinary people, women, and slaves, and were skeptical of gods and traditional morality, making them very popular in later times.

The ancient Greek tragedian Sophocles died in Athens at about age 90. He wrote 123 plays and won 24 victories. His masterpieces 'Oedipus Rex' and 'Antigone' are considered exemplars of Greek tragedy. His innovations included adding a third actor, improving stage setting, and deepening character development.

The Spartan admiral Lysander surprised the Athenian fleet at Aegospotami (near the Hellespont) while the crews were ashore foraging, capturing about 170 ships; only 9 escaped. The Athenian prisoners were all executed. After this battle, Athens had no navy left, and its naval supremacy and grain supply line were cut off for good.

v.t: Rome begins the siege of Veii
v.s: The Roman Republic began a ten-year siege (405-396 BC) of the Etruscan city of Veii. This was one of the most important early external conquests of Rome; after its fall, Veii's territory was annexed, and the Roman army began to receive pay.

v.t: End of the Peloponnesian War – Athens surrenders
v.s: After the Battle of Aegospotami, Athens, starved of supplies, surrendered to Sparta. Sparta imposed a pro-Spartan oligarchic government, the 'Thirty Tyrants', tore down the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus, dissolved the Delian League, and reduced the Athenian navy to 12 ships. The Peloponnesian War ended, with Sparta the hegemon of Greece. The war had exhausted the entire Greek world, paving the way for the rise of Macedon.

v.t: Cyrus the Younger contests the Persian throne
v.s: Persian Emperor Darius II died, and his eldest son Artaxerxes II succeeded. His younger son Cyrus the Younger, supported by his mother Parysatis, mustered an army including over 10,000 Greek mercenaries (the 'Ten Thousand'), to challenge for the throne.

The Thirty Tyrants, supported by Sparta, imposed a reign of terror in Athens, executing about 1,500 democrats and exiling thousands. They deprived most citizens of property and political rights. Democratic exiles gathered in Thebes and prepared to counterattack.

v.t: Partition of Jin – Warring States period formally begins
v.s: The three great ministers of Jin – Zhao, Wei, and Han – partitioned the state of Jin, and King Weilie of Zhou formally enfeoffed Zhao Ji, Wei Si, and Han Qian as feudal lords. The destruction of Jin marked the end of the Spring and Autumn period and the beginning of the Warring States period. Thereafter, wars among vassal states became more frequent and intense, eventually forming the Seven Powers: Qi, Chu, Yan, Han, Zhao, Wei, and Qin.

Thrasybulus, the democratic leader, led exiles from Thebes into Athens, defeated the army of the Thirty Tyrants, and overthrew their rule. Under the arbitration of the Spartan king Pausanias, Athens restored democracy and passed an 'amnesty decree' forbidding prosecution for political crimes during the oligarchy (except for a few leaders). Athenian democracy was restored, but greatly weakened.

Sparta attacked Elis, accusing it of treaty violations and insulting Sparta. After three years of war, Sparta forced Elis to submit, deprived it of some of its privileges in organizing the Olympic Games, and demolished its walls. Sparta's hegemonic behavior aroused dissatisfaction among other city-states.

v.t: Battle of Cunaxa and the March of the Ten Thousand
v.s: The Greek mercenaries (about 13,000) and Asian troops of Cyrus the Younger fought the army of Artaxerxes II at Cunaxa, near Babylon. Although the Greeks routed the Persian left wing, Cyrus was killed while charging. After the battle, the Greek generals were treacherously killed, and the soldiers, led by Xenophon, fought their way back to Greece through Persian territory, a march of about 2,500 km through Armenia and along the Black Sea coast. Xenophon wrote the 'Anabasis' about this event.

Wu Qi, a native of the state of Wei (Zuo's), studied military art in Lu and became a general of Lu. In 412 BC, when Qi attacked Lu, Wu Qi led the Lu army to a great victory over Qi. But the Lu nobles, suspicious because Wu Qi's wife was from Qi, forced him to leave. He then went to Wei, where he became a leading general under Marquis Wen of Wei and created the 'Wu Wei Warriors' (elite heavy infantry).
